serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of stir-fried minced chicken with Thai basil weighs approximately 400 g, typically served over steamed jasmine rice as a main course.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, stir-fried (NDB No. 05062) and Thai basil chicken dish component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai basil minced chicken stir-fry exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central stir-fried chicken entries (FDC ID 171477 and related) combined with HPB Singapore nutrient data for comparable stir-fried chicken dishes. Input record values (energy 137.5 kcal, protein 8.75 g, fat 7.5 g, carbohydrate 8.75 g, sodium 450 mg per 100 g) are consistent with USDA and HPB reference ranges for this dish type and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 450 mg/100 g is plausible given fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce content.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on mixed stir-fried chicken and vegetable dish with moderate carbohydrate from sauce components.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai basil minced chicken stir-fry. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed stir-fried chicken dishes with sauce components from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with moderate carbohydrate (8.75 g/100 g) from oyster sauce, fish sauce, and any residual starch. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving (35 g from 400 g portion) / 100 = 15.75.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 450 mg per 100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, or ask for sauces on the side, to lower sodium intake below 400 mg per 100 g per DASH dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 8.75 g per 100 g with estimated GI of 45 (low); sugar is low at 2.5 g per 100 g, but sauce components may contribute hidden sugars.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this dish in moderate portions as the low GI and high protein content (8.75 g/100 g) moderate glycaemic response, but should verify curry paste and oyster sauce for added sugar content and monitor total carbohydrate intake per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 450 mg per 100 g is elevated; saturated fat is moderate at 2.0 g per 100 g and trans fat is present at 0.05 g per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to reduce sodium and saturated fat load, request low-sodium sauce alternatives, and ensure the dish is prepared with minimal added oil to keep total fat within heart-healthy targets per AHA guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 450 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg threshold; dish may contain nightshade vegetables (chilli, eggplant) that can aggravate solanine-sensitive RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients with solanine sensitivity should avoid or substitute nightshade ingredients such as chilli and eggplant, and request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce to lower sodium below 400 mg per 100 g to minimise inflammatory risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Minced Chicken with Thai Basil (ผัดกะเพราไก่สับ, Phat Kaphrao Kai Sap) is a popular Thai stir-fried dish made from finely minced chicken cooked in a hot wok with Thai holy basil (kra pao), garlic, fresh chilli, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce. The dish is characterised by its bold, savoury, and slightly spicy flavour profile with aromatic herbal notes from the Thai basil. The texture is moist and tender with a glossy sauce coating the minced meat. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as a main course over steamed jasmine rice or with a fried egg on top. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 137.5 kcal, 8.75 g protein, 7.5 g fat (of which 2.0 g is saturated), and 8.75 g carbohydrate with 2.5 g sugar and 1.25 g dietary fibre. Sodium content is high at 450 mg per 100 g due to the multi-sauce seasoning base. The dish has a low estimated glycaemic index of approximately 45, making it suitable for moderate consumption by individuals managing blood glucose. However, the high sodium content makes it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients, who should request reduced sauce quantities. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker and restaurant contexts and is dairy-free. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the chicken, fish sauce, and oyster sauce content.

Nutrient values are adapted from USDA stir-fried chicken entries and are consistent with the input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable mixed stir-fried chicken dishes. Medium confidence is appropriate given the absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Thai basil minced chicken stir-fry preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. Input record had duplicate portion entries (two identical '1 plate / 400 g' entries); deduplicated to one serving. Input food_subgroup was 'Noodle dishes' but the dish as described contains no noodles; corrected to 'Stir-fried meat dishes'. Input aliases array was empty; populated with multilingual and romanized aliases per Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules. The dish is sold under the menu code '501D' on GrabFood Singapore. Halal status retained from input dietary_tags. Trans fat at 0.05 g per 100 g is a trace level consistent with minor cooking oil contribution.
